title: Cellular Immune Response to Rhinovirus
content:
When human tissue cultivated in a laboratory is exposed to a cold virus, researchers observe that only a small fraction of cells actually become infected. According to Ellen Foxman, a professor of immunobiology at Yale School of Medicine, this mirrors the asymptomatic or mild colds often seen in humans. Even though only about 1% of the cells are infected, the entire tissue changes its behavior to fight off the threat. The infected cells release warning molecules known as interferons, which prompt neighboring cells to activate their antiviral defenses. As a result, the virus is contained and prevented from spreading beyond the initial infection site.

However, scientists wondered what would happen if this critical interferon signal failed to activate. When researchers blocked the warning signal using a drug, the tissue began to ooze mucus and provoked severe inflammation. A delay of just 24 hours in recognizing the infection allows the rhinovirus to multiply rapidly before neighboring cells can mount a defense. Consequently, when the cells finally respond, there is a much larger viral presence to fight, leading to significantly more damaging effects. These findings highlight the importance of the initial immune response and could pave the way for developing targeted treatments or even a universal vaccine for the common cold.
